grounding_source_rows: review rows 252, 420 (immigration context); taxonomy International Law descriptions
grounding_note: Deportation (removal defense) versus General Immigration/Visas (affirmative applications) is the sharpest posture split in immigration practice: has the person received a Notice to Appear or removal order? That paper fact converts a paperwork matter into time-critical defense. Users describe their goal ('stay with my family', 'fix my papers') without stating the procedural posture.

scenarios.opening_query: I need an immigration lawyer. My situation with my papers has gotten complicated.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: International Law > General Immigration/Visas || International Law > Deportation
scenarios.hidden_fact: I received a Notice to Appear in immigration court — removal proceedings have started against me.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: I got a Notice to Appear — removal proceedings have started.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether removal proceedings have been initiated
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: No court involvement — my green card renewal was rejected for a missing document and I need it refiled properly.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: No court — my green card renewal bounced over a missing document and needs refiling.
scenarios.vetting_note: NTA receipt is the single decisive posture fact in immigration routing.

scenarios.opening_query: My husband's work visa situation fell apart when he lost his job and we're scared about what happens next.
scenarios.hidden_fact: His authorized stay lapsed months ago and ICE sent him a notice to appear after a traffic stop.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: His status lapsed months ago, and after a traffic stop ICE sent him a notice to appear.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the person is out of status and in proceedings
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: He's still within his grace period — we need a new employer petition or a status change filed quickly.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: He's still in the grace period — we need a new petition or status change filed fast.
scenarios.vetting_note: Grace-period vs proceedings is the fork every laid-off visa holder faces; family voice realistic.

scenarios.opening_query: They're trying to deport my brother and our whole family is falling apart over it.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: International Law > Deportation || Criminal Law > Post Conviction/Appeals
scenarios.hidden_fact: The removal case is entirely built on his old guilty plea — his defense lawyer never warned him it was deportable, and we're told vacating that plea is the path.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It all rests on his old guilty plea — his lawyer never warned him it was deportable. We're told the plea has to be vacated first.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether attacking an underlying conviction is the operative remedy
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: There's no conviction involved — it's a visa-overstay case, and his hearing is in six weeks.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: No conviction — it's an overstay case with a hearing in six weeks.
scenarios.vetting_note: Padilla-style plea vacatur is a real crimmigration route; the plea's role is the disclosure.

scenarios.opening_query: I want to bring my mother here from overseas so she can live with us. What does that take?
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: International Law > General Immigration/Visas || International Law > Deportation
scenarios.hidden_fact: She's actually already here — she overstayed a tourist visa years ago and was just referred to immigration court after an asylum office interview.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: She's already here — overstayed a tourist visa years back, and the asylum office just referred her to immigration court.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the parent is abroad awaiting a petition or already here in proceedings
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: She's in her home country waiting — this is a fresh family petition and consular visa process.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: She's back home waiting — it's a fresh family petition through the consulate.
scenarios.vetting_note: Families frame removal cases as 'bringing mom here'; physical location plus court referral flips the posture entirely.

scenarios.opening_query: My wife is not a citizen and our divorce is turning ugly. I'm worried about her more than me.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Family Law > General (Divorce/Separation etc.) || International Law > General Immigration/Visas
scenarios.hidden_fact: Her green card is conditional based on our marriage — she needs the immigration side handled so the divorce doesn't cost her the card.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: Her green card is conditional on our marriage — she needs the immigration side protected before the divorce finishes.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether conditional immigration status depends on the marriage
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: She's a naturalized citizen — immigration isn't a factor; the fight is the house and support.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: She's naturalized — immigration isn't an issue. The fight is the house and support.
scenarios.vetting_note: Row 252's conditional-status pattern: humans disagreed on whether immigration was context or a service need.

scenarios.opening_query: My employee is one of my best people and now there's a problem with his ability to keep working here.
scenarios.hidden_fact: He confided that he has a final removal order from years ago that just resurfaced — ICE contacted him directly.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: He told me he has an old final removal order that resurfaced — ICE contacted him.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether a removal order already exists
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It's a work-authorization renewal backlog — his EAD expired while the renewal sits in processing.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's the renewal backlog — his work permit expired while the renewal is stuck in processing.
scenarios.vetting_note: Employer-side framing, employee's posture fact; EAD backlog vs final order are opposite worlds.

scenarios.opening_query: We adopted our daughter abroad and something is wrong with finalizing everything now that we're home.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Family Law > Interstate/Independent Adoption || International Law > General Immigration/Visas
scenarios.hidden_fact: The adoption itself is final — the problem is her citizenship paperwork; USCIS rejected the N-600 over the visa category she entered on.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The adoption's final — it's her citizenship papers. USCIS rejected the N-600 over her entry visa category.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the remaining issue is the adoption or the child's immigration status
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: Her immigration status is fine — the foreign decree needs to be re-adopted/recognized in our state court to get a birth certificate.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: Her status is fine — we need the foreign decree recognized in state court for her birth certificate.
scenarios.vetting_note: International adoption splits into family-court and USCIS problems; which document failed is the fact.

scenarios.opening_query: I've lived here since I was two years old and something from when I was 19 is now putting everything at risk.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: International Law > Deportation || Criminal Law > Expunction
scenarios.hidden_fact: I'm actually a citizen — derived through my dad's naturalization — so deportation isn't the issue; the old conviction blocking my security-clearance job needs expungement.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: Turns out I'm a citizen through my dad's naturalization — so it's not deportation. I need the old conviction expunged for my clearance job.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: the user's citizenship status and what the old record actually threatens
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: I'm a green card holder, and DHS says that old conviction makes me removable — I have a hearing notice.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: I'm a green card holder and DHS says the old conviction makes me removable — I have a hearing notice.
scenarios.vetting_note: Derived citizenship is a genuinely overlooked fact that dissolves removal exposure entirely.
